author: 𝕁𝕒𝕪
content:
Bro, you lost $30k over 5 years. That’s $6k a year. $500’a month. $125 a week. $75 a day.  

Look at it as your 5 year tuition to learn practice and perfect.  

Are you trading to earn a living. Is it a hobby or are you trading solely to get your losses back ? 

Figure out why you’re trading. You can’t make up 5 years in 1 day or 1 week.  Has it been done in a week or a month SURE. But if we get stuck in a mindset we know is wrong yet are only aware of it after we go against it then the losses are gone and there was no tuition. It was essentially gambling.  

I been there, believe me. I’ve blown $35k in less than 2 months.  I feel your pain. A lot of us do.  

If you make $75 a day and shut her down you will get your losses back in time.  
You’re going to have good days and bad obviously but if your RM is there and you’re actually journaling and going over your journal weekly at least it’s going to make it.  

Anyone can make a trade, we have to find trades.  
All Love Bro. Hope you’re able to get a handle on it all and breathe.

author: Kam
content:
hey <@1323448182851637345>, I'm assuming your trade was a BnR off the 5m opening range. a few points to consider: 

1) on higher timeframes we opened up right in the middle of yesterday's range on the ES, so in order to go long or short at an intraday level like the 5m OR, you ideally need stronger evidence of momentum in either direction. Try zooming out to the 15m or 1hr timeframe to see the bigger picture 

2) the amount of displacement is key for the BnR system. In this case there wasn't a strong push or super strong candle close above the level you're using for the retest. Note the upper wicks (a tell for sellers) on the push above the 5m range on the 1m chart

3) if your entry was on the 9:57 candle, it's likely you didn't wait until the candle closed for confirmation that your level was holding. Remember you want to see strong price action at your level in order to enter. 

Will post a couple of links to videos Jack has made recently that may help with building higher timeframe narratives for your trade ideas as well as good entry confirmation signals

author: Bhargav
content:
From $500 to $7,876 – My Journey of Patience, Discipline, and Belief

Hey everyone,

I wanted to take a moment to share something close to my heart – not to boast, but to hopefully inspire anyone who's feeling stuck or uncertain in their trading journey.

I joined this trading community about a year and a half ago. Back then, I was just eager to learn, hungry for growth, and like many of you, figuring things out one step at a time. It wasn’t always smooth. There were days filled with doubt, losses that made me question everything, and moments when I wondered if this journey was really for me.

But I kept showing up.

I studied, practiced, journaled my trades, learned from my mistakes, and leaned on this community for support and accountability. Every win and every loss taught me something valuable – not just about trading, but about myself.

In June, I decided to take on a personal challenge: turn $500 into $5,000. Not only did I hit that milestone, but I ended the challenge with a $7,876 account.

Yes, the numbers are exciting – but what I’m truly proud of is the mindset it took to get there: patience, consistency, emotional control, and unwavering belief.

This journey has shown me that it’s not about overnight success – it’s about persistence, process, and trusting that your time will come if you don't give up.

To anyone in this community who’s still grinding, still learning, still waiting for that breakthrough: keep going. You’re closer than you think.

Much love and gratitude to all of you who've been part of this path. The journey is far from over — let’s keep growing, together. Big Thanks to <@717554586843611217> , <@801169196015943731> and all the Mentors here.

– Bhargav

author: Brute7
content:
It’s officially been one month since I started my intraday trading journey — and it all began with inspiration from the Scarface YouTube channel. What started as simple curiosity quickly turned into a daily commitment to grow, learn, and show up — regardless of profits or losses.

Like many beginners, I dove into breakout, retest, and price action strategies, thinking I had a solid handle on options trading. But over time, I realized there’s so much more to it — market news, economic reports, index correlations, relative strength, option Greeks, and key levels all play a critical role. One of my early mistakes was spreading my attention too thin, tracking too many tickers without focusing deeply on any.

So three days ago, I made a shift: I committed to just five tickers — SPX, NVDA, TSLA, QQQ, and SPY. Since then, I’ve seen a clear difference. Studying these names daily has helped me understand their behavior better, enter trades with more precision, and exit with more confidence.

Today, I hit my personal all-time high in trading. It’s not $10k (yet), but I know I’m on the right path — one day at a time.

author: John H
content: I don't think someone else's contract size matters. We have to size to our own accounts and the risk we're willing to take with each trade. For example, your risk might be $100 where Jack & Tony may be at $1000.  So, think in terms of if they bought 50 option contracts, they could only lose $.20 before they hit their max loss. If you wanted $.20 of risk equaling $100 then you could only buy 5 contracts. And, $.20 can happen in a split second. That's why knowing where you are getting out, and sizing appropriately to the number of contracts you can buy is important. Your stop might be at $.50, which would then mean only 2 contracts. Hope this makes sense. At the end of the day, it's not apples to apples to try and compare our trade size to what they are doing.

author: Kam
content:
Hey Sean - this is a great question, and one I’ve spent a lot of time thinking about recently.

Honestly, it sounds like you’re doing a lot of the right things already. You’ve built structure around risk, setup clarity, journaling, and daily reflection - really solid foundation.

When I think about consistency, I try to break it down into a few core lenses (credit <@717554586843611217> <@801169196015943731> <@1014389514141184074> and Mark Douglas!)

Are your best setups clearly defined and **repeatable**?

Are you avoiding trades outside your system - even when they “look good”?

Are you managing risk the same way every time - not just when you're confident, but when you're unsure too?

You mentioned you’ve been trading this way for ~6 months. One thing I’ve been using personally is a ‘trading block’ model - aiming for 20 trades aligned with my process out of every 25. It helps me stay honest about process over PnL. If I hit it, I earn the right to pay myself with a small size bump. If not, I reset. It’s been a helpful way to compound responsibly as I've had issues sizing up too quickly in the past. 

25 trades for me typically maps to about a month’s worth of trading, but the calendar isn’t the point - the reps are.

So yeah IMO, it's not about waiting X months or years. It’s about proving to yourself that your process is repeatable. Hope that helps!

author: Hayden
content:
- Candle Close Stop Loss

Pros:

Filters out “wick hunts” and intraday noise.

Keeps you in trades that recover quickly after a fake-out.

Helps align with structure rather than random ticks.

Cons:

Risk can be larger than expected, since by the time the candle closes, the option premium may be down much more.

Hard to execute in options intraday because option premiums are extremely sensitive (gamma, delta shifts). A stock moving $0.30 against you can crush the premium more than expected.

Can lead to hesitating, hoping the candle “saves” the trade, which sometimes just means deeper loss.



- Hard Stop at Exact Price

Pros:

Clear, mechanical, no decision fatigue.

Protects capital better, especially in options where theta + gamma magnify losses quickly.

Removes emotional “what if it comes back?”

Cons:

You’ll get wicked out more often, especially intraday.

Slippage can hurt — a 10–20¢ slip in stock can translate into 20–30% option loss.

Misses some trades that recover immediately after the fake-out.



- Slippage Reality in Options

Options don’t move smoothly with the underlying; spreads + illiquidity + IV shifts = exits are rarely clean.

Market orders at stop get filled worse than stock.

Sometimes you get slipped on size even if spreads look tight.

This is why many intraday options traders actually prefer mental stops or alerts and exit manually, because hard stops can trigger at the worst possible premium.


Simplification: If you hesitate a lot on your stops, you have a small account, use exact stop levels. (Manual or Orders) The BNR usually works right away or it doesn't at all so most of the time (In my opinion) its worth it to close out. Tony and Jack have a lot higher risk tolerance than most and have been trading price action for many years.

If you wait for closures - accept the risk you're R/R might be skewed on a lot of your trades but your win % might be higher (if they reverse), better for experienced traders

author: Jerry
content:
Hey guys,

Wanted to share my experience of going from struggling to profitable (just a few months so far, but still). Maybe it can be a good starting point for some of you—it really changed the game for me.
I struggled a lot before. At one point, I was trading 20 TSLA contracts, taking small wins but then wiping everything out with big losses. Ended up down almost $15k in just a few months. That’s when I stopped and decided to really think.
I built an Excel table with all of Tony’s trades this year, split by symbol. Every row was a trade with entry (I added some delay since I’m slower), stop loss, max potential win until SL was hit, etc. I kept adding parameters and I’m still revisiting them, improving execution.
From that analysis, I found the main thing that killed my profitability: scaling out too early. I used to sell 30% on the HOD pop, then more on the next big candle, and my RR was completely ruined.
So I made a script in Excel to simulate different scenarios and find the best monthly outcome. The result was simple: don’t scale at all until TSLA moves at least $4 (around 2R), then exit. That’s it.
That one change completely flipped my trading. Now I trade 10 contracts and make way more than I did before with 20. This is just the first step, of course. Next is working on dynamic sizing based on setup quality, scaling in/out properly, etc. But this shift was the key—it stopped the bleeding and got me back to making money.

Hope it will help

author: Jerry
content:
Yeah. Doing your own analysis is essential. Only that way you will get confidence. I simulated different appproaches.
1. No scaling. That gives the best results - best RR and 50% win rate
2. 1 scale. Again tried to find an optimum with simulation where and how many to scale to improve win rate at the price of RR. Managed to do it. Increased to 65% win rate with some combinations but it hit the total profit.
3. 1 scale + runners. Same results as in second approach.

So after all simulations no scaling approach gives the best profit, though much more complicated from mental perspective. 

To the question why Tony manages to have such great result - i see 3 points
1. Much better entry so good RR on HOD
2. Scaling based on good reading of price action like scaling out when several wicks as a sign of weakness
3. Scaling out base on context and not on predefined structure.

But all these points come with experience. We will get there someday

author: G Nice
content: Hey guys, for small accounts (1-2k) or those just starting out. I would highly recommend trading shares instead of options until you have a solid setup/trading plan established. You will have far less anxiety trading shares as it doesn’t move as fast as options (a $1 drop in share price will be a $1 P&L loss/share. where an ATM options contract is typically around $50 loss/con). You can still trade the B&R live with the group and practice your entry, scales and SL as if they are options except it will not blow your account. Do this until you are consistently profitable and have a solid trading plan. The point is not the amount of money you make in shares but mainly to establish a solid scalable trading plan that you can transfer over to options when you are ready. I hate to see/hear people blowing their accounts or losing more than they can manage so I hope this helps.

author: 305 Trader
content:
Here is a really sinple method I use as an options trader for scalping,

a)- What is the max loss per trade I am willing to accept today given my account size?

b)- when the set up is forming, I watch where my TPs are, I take a TP1 and also the ultimate, TP1 to me should be 1:2, the ultimate is whatever it can be. Understand this is a super conservative  (but game changer) approach. 

c) - Here is the science, what is the underlying (real stock) $cents size of the most realistic stop loss (not an arbitrary one)??
d)
Formula: 

 c) x contracts number x 0.5 = a). 

Example : Stop loss measures  25 cts of stock value. 
Max loss per trade is $50
Factor is 0.5
So,
25 x 0.5 = 12.5
$50 / 12.5 = 4 contracts. 

That is your contract size to lose $50 as per your measured stop loss (as long as you exit when it says so). 

then, 

Your RvR  ratio to TP1 is 1:2
So,

You have to measure  and find 50 stock value cents to TP1 to enter the trade.

Notice I measure 1:2 RvR to TP1, yes I know this is conservative, but not really cause you are only interested anyways in a  small stop loss, so provided this is what you look for, a RvR to TP1 of 1:2 is realistic and it will occur often. 

Hope this helps, with this method you will be more focused on the  size of your stop loss rather than % of your account to discard set ups that are not winners in what you need: Lower risk

author: Dan Grand
content:
Hey everyone, I wanted to share something important that I realized about my trading journey, and I’m hoping to get some guidance and support from the community.

Back in August, I had an amazing month trading options — one of my best ever. That gave me the confidence to try trading with real money. My first week was +$800, the second week I lost -$1000, and that’s when I realized I didn’t truly understand risk the way I thought I did. I stepped back and tried futures for a while, but trading futures alone without a mentor has been difficult. Watching YouTube isn’t enough when you don’t have someone to ask real questions.

And now, after thinking deeply, I’ve decided not to give up on options. I want to continue trading options seriously, because this is the environment where I feel the most support and structure, and where I’ve already seen my best results. I just want to do it the right way this time.

One big realization I had is this:

I can follow rules.
I can take clean entries.
I can respect my stop.
I can let my trades hit either TP or SL.

But what I cannot do well yet is read the higher-timeframe context and understand when the market wants to move long and when it wants to move short. That’s the piece I’ve never deeply worked on.

Sometimes I take a valid entry according to my rules, but I’m trading it completely against the larger direction — while some of you or the mentors are actually looking for trades in the opposite direction because of context. This made me realize I’m not just “pressing buttons.” I’m missing a whole dimension of the market.

So as I return to trading options, I really want to improve this part of my trading.
If anyone can share advice or help me build a simple, structured plan for learning how to read higher-timeframe context, it would mean a lot.

Thanks in advance for any support or suggestions. I appreciate this community and I’m ready to grow properly this time.

author: Hayden
content:
Sure 👍 

Truth is, numbers alone don’t show the full story. They don’t show the bad trades I passed on, the discipline it took to sit on my hands, or the weeks where protecting capital mattered more than making it. Mostly important is the reasons why 

P&L can create the wrong focus, invite comparison, and push people into forcing trades just to “keep up"

But I also believe in transparency. Everyday I do coaching and talk about my setups / PNL / scaling and why I didn't take trades. If you really want something that's going to help, those sessions and thoughts will go a lot further than numbers will 😅

author: Hayden
content:
For me, I generally risk anywhere from $600–$900 per trade and aim for $1,200–$1,500+ per win at minimum. My position size usually sits around the $8k–$12k range

It definitely wasn’t always like this though. I spent a long time consistently risking $50 to make $100. Slow, steady growth and scaling the account over time is really the only sustainable way to do this 👍
